sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=40, col=13), UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 33, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Green entity at rows 30-32 with WHITE center. Green entity at rows 39-41 with CHARCOAL center. Dark frame empty. Moving charcoal-center entity DOWN through dark frame changes it to white. Need to change the other entity too.
- Goal model: Get both green entities to have white centers by passing them through the dark frame.
- Action model: Arrow keys move selected entity by 3 cells, blocked by purple bar.
- Plan: Select charcoal-center entity at rows 39-41 and move it UP 2 times through the dark frame.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 32 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current state:
- Row 30: NNN (green)
- Row 31: NcN (green with charcoal center)
- Row 32: NNN (green)
- Row 33: WWW (white)
- Row 34: NWN (green with white center)
- Row 35: NNN (green)
So there are TWO green entities visible:
1. At rows 30-32 with charcoal center
2. At rows 33-35 with white center (inside the dark frame)
But the segmentation only shows one green entity (id=6) at rows 30-32 with charcoal center. The entity inside the dark frame is split into white and green parts again.
Wait, this is the same state as before! When I moved the charcoal-center entity UP from below, it ended up inside the dark frame with a white center (transformed), and the other entity (which had white center) was pushed out to rows 30-32 but now has a charcoal center.
So the pattern is:
- When an entity enters the dark frame, it gets transformed (charcoal center → white center)
- The entity that was previously in the frame gets pushed out and loses its white center (becomes charcoal)
This means I can't have both entities with white centers at the same time using this mechanic. The dark frame can only hold one entity at a time.
Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is NOT to have both entities with white centers. Maybe it's something else.
Looking at the right panel, there's another dark frame at rows 26-30, cols 44-48, with a white interior. Maybe I need to move an entity to THAT frame?
But the purple bar blocks movement between the left and right panels. So how can I get an entity to the right panel?
Wait, let me look at the initial state again. The right panel has a dark frame with a white interior. Maybe the goal is to have the left dark frame also have a white interior (which it does now - rows 33-35 are white).
Actually, looking at the ascii:
- Left dark frame interior: rows 33-35, cols 12-14
  - Row 33: WWW (white)
  - Row 34: NWN (green with white center)
  - Row 35: NNN (green)
So the interior is NOT all white - there's a green entity inside.
- Right dark frame interior: rows 27-29, cols 45-47
  - This is id=5, color=w, 9 pixels - all white
So the right dark frame has a white interior (empty), and the left dark frame has a green entity inside.
Maybe the goal is to have BOTH dark frames have green entities inside? Or maybe the goal is to have the left dark frame empty (white interior) like the right one?
Let me think about this differently. The right panel has a dark frame with a white interior. This might be the "target" state - an empty dark frame. The left panel has a dark frame that I need to make empty (white interior).
Currently, the left dark frame has a green entity inside. If I move that entity out, the dark frame interior becomes white.
Let me try moving the entity inside the dark frame OUT (either UP or DOWN).
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the state. The entity at rows 33-35 is inside the dark frame. The entity at rows 30-32 is above the dark frame. If I select the entity at rows 30-32 and move it DOWN, it will enter the dark frame and push the other entity out.
But I've been doing this cycle and it just swaps the entities. Let me try a different approach.
What if I need to move the entity to the RIGHT dark frame? But the purple bar blocks that.
Or what if I need to click on something specific? Let me look at the valid actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Maybe I need to MOUSE click on the right dark frame to "activate" it or something?
Let me try clicking on the right dark frame's interior.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Left dark frame has green entity inside (white center). Entity above has charcoal center. Right dark frame is empty (white interior). Moving entities through dark frame transforms charcoal→white center but pushes out existing entity. Can't have both white at once.
Goal model: Maybe need to interact with the right dark frame, or need to empty the left dark frame.
Plan: Try clicking on the right dark frame to see if it does something.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the right dark frame's interior
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 46}])

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("After click on right frame (rows 25-32, cols 42-50):")
for i in range(25, 33):
    print(f"  row {i}: {ascii_lines[i][42:50]}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAll nodes in right panel:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    if b and b[0][1] >= 35:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={b}, children={n['children']}")

print(f"\nResult: {last_action_result}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
